title: Bothersomely Improbable Events in NGE
wikitext:
[]: Bothersomely Improbable Events in NGE             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Oct 10, 2008 1:50 pm Not to be confused with implausible events (i.e. Shinji's clothes being pseudo-substantiated or Kaworu not realizing that "Adam" is Lilith*), these are things that happened in Neon Genesis Evangelion that were inexcusably unlikely considering the show's internal mechanics. In other words, things that pulled me out of the show because they were meant to be dramatic, but mainly left me thinking something along the lines of "What are the odds of that happening?". -In episode three, Kensuke and Toji are subjected to the "finger treatment", as I have called it in the past. They watch Shamshel fighting Unit 01, who is flung towards them... SPLAT! Not on them, not next to them, but in just the right position so that Kensuke and Toji are right between her fingers . Shamshel had to fling Unit 01 in exactly the right direction at exactly the right velocity to land in such a manner. I found this to be so unlikely that I was hoping this scene would be altered for Rebuild. It wasn't. -In episode eighteen, Misato and Ritsuko are inside the building that explodes when Unit 03 is possessed by Bardiel. Voice overs make it seem like there are very few survivors, but Misato and Ritsuko just got off with minor injuries. It is actually mentioned how lucky Misato was, and Ritsuko mentions that her own injuries aren't as bad as Misato's. -In the very next episode, since Shinji is no longer a pilot during Zeruel's attack, he is sent to an evacuation shelter. Zeruel's fight with Unit 02 ends with the latter being decapitated. Her head is flung up, up and away, to crash right into the evacuation shelter where Shinji happens to be . Not just that, but according to a voice over the impact apparently killed several people and quite likely injured several others, but it landed just in the right place so that Shinji would be staring right at it, but not be in any way injured by the crash. This one's way more improbable than the last one, even. In terms of story progression, it's quite important, but that makes it more bothersome to me that the show relied on something so astronomically improbable for Shinji to decide to pilot Unit 01 again. -In End of Evangelion , almost everyone at NERV is killed by the JSSDF without any hesitation. However, when those military guys reach Shinji, the man in charge of executing him calls a superior to state that he has found the Third Child(ren) and is "commencing termination" (of course he doesn't bother to kill him first and then report!), aims the gun at Shinji's head, waits a bit, coldly says that it's "nothing personal", waits a bit more, and then gets shot by Misato. This was, in my opinion, the worst thing about EoE. Misato arrived at exactly the last possible second. I feel that it would have been more effective (and less cliché) to have the JSSDF guy say that he is "commencing termina-" only to be shot in the head by Misato in mid sentence. Are these things forgivable? I remember certain people who love NGE complaining about certain plot points in Elfen Lied relying too much on certain improbable events, but I feel that a couple of the events I mentioned here are way, way more unlikely. *I actually don't think that the Adam-Lilith confusion is an error at all, and that I solved this a while back . The only apparent leap in my claim was that Kaworu would have the same amnesia when reincarnated that Rei always had, which would seem to me to be likely anyway due to precedent. But this post isn't about that, so I'm not going to talk about it any more here.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri Oct 10, 2008 2:00 pm The first is drammatic effect, the rest is Narrativium.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Fri Oct 10, 2008 2:20 pm i treat the "clothes forming in episode 20" thing as purely a miscommunication due to the hectic animation schedule, that the Japanese dub actors than tried to grace over and explain when they realized it didn't match episode 19

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Oct 10, 2008 2:28 pm V wrote: i treat the "clothes forming in episode 20" thing as purely a miscommunication due to the hectic animation schedule, that the Japanese dub actors than tried to grace over and explain when they realized it didn't match episode 19

[]:             Quote Post by The Bastard King » Fri Oct 10, 2008 2:35 pm Killing a designated kid in a school uniform with a known history of saving people might merit more of an emotional response then killing a nameless person in a uniform, even if it's only enough to inform them that you're a prick. All other answers can probably be drawn from the laws of anime physics.

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Fri Oct 10, 2008 2:53 pm Consider that the odds of someone being born in this universe are stacked against the odds of them having never existed, and all stories, even true stories, become ridiculously contrived and improbable. Consider that before you start complaining about improbable narrative events.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Oct 10, 2008 2:58 pm Formless One wrote: Consider that the odds of someone being born in this universe are stacked against the odds of them having never existed, and all stories, even true stories, become ridiculously contrived and improbable. Consider that before you start complaining about improbable narrative events.

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Fri Oct 10, 2008 3:14 pm That's a logical fallacy right there.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Fri Oct 10, 2008 3:31 pm Chill the hell out, it's an imaginary story about Freudbots, god-clones, gay celestial beings, and reality-warping emofags. Get over it.

[]:             Quote Post by Defectron » Fri Oct 10, 2008 4:03 pm Any of Misatos plans actually working would count as implausible.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Oct 10, 2008 4:11 pm Formless One wrote: I was always talking about specific people.

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Fri Oct 10, 2008 5:15 pm You don't seem to understand. Any given person existing is not implausible because they have just as much of a chance of existing as anyone else, and some one person will always be there. Unit 02's head landing in the exact right place that it needed to is implausible because the factors of random human generation are not involved.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaiku » Fri Oct 10, 2008 5:21 pm WOuld you count the scene in EoE with the Flame Thrower in the English dub. WHere the JSSD soldier hits the guy they scream, he stops and then he flames him again and he screams again. Wouldn't he just be screaming the whole way through?

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Oct 10, 2008 5:32 pm Mmkay, I suppose I should redefine what I was saying to "implausibly improbable" instead of just "improbable" or "implausible". Yes, the likelihood of any given thing occurring is improbable, but not improbable in an implausible manner as one of those things must happen. Zaiku: That flamethrower thing was just an example of one of those implausible things that probability doesn't really play much of a factor in. Also, the existence of Earthlike planets isn't improbable at all. Research some sources not written/funded by creationists.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaiku » Fri Oct 10, 2008 5:37 pm Zaiku: That flamethrower thing was just an example of one of those implausible things that probability doesn't really play much of a factor in.

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Fri Oct 10, 2008 5:55 pm Also, the existence of Earthlike planets isn't improbable at all. Research some sources not written/funded by creationists.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Oct 10, 2008 6:09 pm (I grumble at the fact that I'm derailing my own thread..) Yes, I'm aware of the Fermi paradox, but it's baseless. Notice the Wikipedia refers to it as an " apparent contradiction". It also offers various solutions to this supposed paradox. Keep in mind that FTL travel is currently thought to be impossible, and under Asimov's postulation for the sake of argument that there are twenty-one technologically advanced civilizations in our galaxy (though he believed the number to be lower), any of those civilizations contacting us, even if we were only a few lightyears away, would be quite unlikely. I don't think that Asimov's essay I mentioned is available online for free anywhere (I've looked in the past), but if you can find his book The Planet That Wasn't , it's one of the essays in there. I recommend reading it if you can. Our existence as it is is improbable, but is not implausible due to the fact that, once intelligent life on Earth started, it would inevitably develop into something . And it did. If something has already happened, then probability is no longer a factor. Probability is useful for determining what can happen now or in the future, or for the sake of hypothetical situations such as a perfect vacuum or a television series.

[]:             Quote Post by HENRCAST » Fri Oct 10, 2008 6:16 pm This kind of improbable stuff happens in every single TV show and movie (specially brazilian soap-operas, but enough about them, since they are disgusting). I mean, what fun would there be if this kind of stuff didn't happen? When the JSSDF soldier is about to kill Shinji you're like that: Are they going to kill Shinji?! When the explosion happens in Ep.18 you don't know if Misato survived, and you keep waiting for the confirmation that she was still alive. When Eva 02's head falls next to Shinji it adds a lots of "OMG Now everything is screwed, the world will end!!" to the episode. But Touji and Kensuke didn't have to be betwenn Eva 01's fingers. Still, it doesn't really make a diference, does it? And about all this existence stuff... Yeah, we are very, very lucky. The chances of live appearing in a certain planet are horribly slim. I mean, if water's molecular geometry (if you haven't learned in school what a molecular geometry/structure is, well...... I won't explain XD) was diferent, life woundn't exist. If the moon didn't exist, we would be screwed. If Earth was only a little closer of farther from the sun we would be screwed. If Earth didn't rotate around itself we would be screwed. The chances really are THAT slim. They are slim to the point of Einstein saying that God exists because otherwise life wouldn't be possible. All that bases on matematic calculations. But we exist, sooooo why does that matter anyway?!

[]:             Quote Post by Formless One » Fri Oct 10, 2008 6:50 pm Just FYI, but part of my point is that improbable events do not make a story implausible. There is a huge distinction. The important part of the paradox is the sheer numbers. The number of planets that will be habitable will be far fewer than the number of planets that are not, no matter how you slice it. In our own solar system, there are eight planets, four dwarf planets, and only one of those sattelites of Sol harbors life. The others are either too small to sustain life (Mars, Venus, all the dwarfs), or are too far away (all the gas giants and their moons). The solutions to the paradox try to solve the reason why any life out there aren't talking to us, but most do not try to say that there are more of them out there then the numbers can back up. The Drake Euation can be tweaked and it gets different numbers, and some people input larger numbers for the number of planets there are out there. But only larger by comparison: they are still small, and do not change the statistical unlikelihood of there being intelligent life for stories to center on.

[]:             Quote Post by NAveryW » Fri Oct 10, 2008 7:04 pm Yes, the vast majority of planets is uninhabitable. However, there are so many planets in so many solar systems that it is extremely unlikely that none of them would support life. HENRCAST: The problem is that when something incredibly unlikely happens just because the story needs it to happen or just because it's more dramatic for it to happen, it comes off to me (and perhaps not the majority of people, but a lot) as more corny than dramatic. In EoE, Shinji's almost getting shot in that manner wasn't as dramatically effective to me as it was corny and cliché.
